draft.title: weather app for runners
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
weather app for runners

Building your first draft…

facade.hypothesis: Users want upbeat encouragement mixed with straightforward data—not sterile, not cutesy
facade.label: Cheerful voice, dry facts
facade.acceptImplies: Design voice should balance motivation with clarity; embrace personality without sacrificing trustworthiness
facade.rejectImplies: Either pursue pure-utility tone (no personality) or lean heavily playful (risk losing credibility on performance data)

facade.hypothesis: Runners prefer a single glanceable number (pace, HR, distance) at top over scrollable detailed stats
facade.axisTargeted: Information density: single key metric vs comprehensive dashboard
facade.label: Live metrics bar
facade.acceptImplies: Design around one prominent live stat with secondary info hidden or minimal; optimize for glance-readability during movement
facade.rejectImplies: User wants full data visibility at once; pursue multi-metric grid or card layout; dense information acceptable if scannable

evidence.hypothesis: Users want upbeat encouragement mixed with straightforward data—not sterile, not cutesy
evidence.implication: Design voice should balance motivation with clarity; embrace personality without sacrificing trustworthiness

facade.hypothesis: For a runner's weather app, reducing visual clutter and showing only essential information (temp, precipitation risk, wind) creates a calm, focused atmosphere that doesn't distract during a run
facade.axisTargeted: organic vs digital — leaning toward clean, restrained digital presence
facade.label: Minimal data overlay
facade.acceptImplies: User values clarity and speed over richness; app should feel purposeful and stripped down; data presentation matters more than visual warmth
facade.rejectImplies: User wants either more visual personality (organic, textured) or richer information density (digital, layered); minimal feels cold or under-designed for their use case

facade.hypothesis: Runners want large, high-contrast typography for quick stat reading during motion
facade.label: Bold sans-serif numbers
facade.acceptImplies: prioritize legible, prominent typeface for pace/distance/time displays; user values clarity over whitespace
facade.rejectImplies: explore serif fonts, smaller text, or more negative space; user prefers softer or more refined visual hierarchy

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimalist, clean aesthetics vs. data-rich visual displays for workout tracking
Whether user wants quick-glance widgets on home screen vs. navigating into detailed weather views
Whether user responds to motivational, encouraging tone vs. straightforward, factual reporting
Whether user wants calm, reassuring interface vs. energetic, action-oriented feel
Whether user prioritizes running-specific metrics (pace impact, humidity) vs. standard weather data
Whether user prefers smooth, gradual transitions vs. snappy, immediate feedback when checking conditions

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers minimalist, clean aesthetics vs. data-rich visual displays for workout tracking
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants quick-glance widgets on home screen vs. navigating into detailed weather views
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to motivational, encouraging tone vs. straightforward, factual reporting
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants calm, reassuring interface vs. energetic, action-oriented feel
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prioritizes running-specific metrics (pace impact, humidity) vs. standard weather data
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers smooth, gradual transitions vs. snappy, immediate feedback when checking conditions
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Runners benefit from velocity-aware scrolling that momentum-glides through weather data but locks onto key metrics (temp, pace zones) rather than free-flowing to dead space
facade.axisTargeted: Scroll behavior: inertial momentum vs. algorithmic snap points
facade.label: Fluid scroll, snap to step
facade.acceptImplies: User values discovery-through-motion and trusting the app to anchor them at useful data thresholds; expect more gesture-driven navigation and physics-based transitions throughout
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers predictable, intentional stepping through information; suggests preference for discrete interactions and instant feedback over fluid momentum—probe next for click-based pagination or tap-to-navigate patterns

facade.hypothesis: A persistent left sidebar housing key metrics (pace, distance, time, temperature, wind) with a toggle to collapse it, keeping the map central but info always accessible
facade.axisTargeted: Navigation structure and information density—whether weather/run data lives in a persistent sidebar or surfaces on demand
facade.label: Sidebar with toggle
facade.acceptImplies: User values quick glance at stats without losing map context; sidebar becomes the primary frame for the layout
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers the map or route view to dominate the full screen; data should appear as floating cards, tabs, or slide-up panels instead

facade.hypothesis: Runners respond better to celebratory pings ("Nice pace!" "New PR zone") than to neutral alerts—personality in real-time feedback builds engagement without feeling gimmicky
facade.axisTargeted: Voice and Personality — Motivational tone vs. Neutral/functional tone
facade.label: Motivational notifications
facade.acceptImplies: Push notifications and in-app alerts should use encouraging, specific language tied to runner achievements; embrace personality in feedback systems
facade.rejectImplies: Runners prefer transactional, emotion-neutral alerts ("Pace alert: 7m 15s"); reserve personality for UI text, not notifications

draft.title: RunWeather
draft.summary: A weather app designed for runners, showing real-time conditions, wind impact, and run-friendly forecasts at a glance.
